mappings.rationaleAI: Culturally recognized union establishing kinship rights (A.1), covering mate selection, sexual behavior, marriage and divorce customs (A.2), and post-marital residence rules (A.3).
Primary unit of social organization encompassing kinship systems (A.1), descent systems (A.4), transmission of economic and political powers (A.5), and the family as an evolving institution (A.6).
Expressive culture including oral traditions, folk beliefs, festivals, and rites of passage, constituting religious belief and folklore as cultural components (D.2).
Visual, literary, and performing arts spanning cross-cultural artistic expression, directly constituting artistic expression as a cultural element (D.4).
Hierarchical social stratification by economic position and cultural background, directly representing organization by status including class systems (B.2).
Structured play for entertainment and competition, a cultural universal present across all human societies, constituting recreation, sports, and games (D.6).
Intentional productive activity with division of labor as a core concept, directly relevant to production, division of labor, and role differentiation in economic systems (C.1.a).
Transmission of knowledge, belief, law, and culture through speech, constituting religious belief and folklore as cultural elements (D.2).
Social domain of production, distribution, and consumption, encompassing economic systems from primitive (C.1) to developed types.
Human practice of pursuing wildlife for food, constituting a primary form of production in primitive economic systems (C.1.a).
Unjustified distinctions based on group membership relevant to organization by status including caste systems and systems of serfdom (B.2).
Cultural universal present in all human societies encompassing performing arts, constituting artistic expression as a major cultural element (D.4).
Enforceable rules regulating conduct, constituting legal systems as a cultural component common to all societies (D.3).
Ownership and coerced exploitation of persons, directly representing systems characterized by slavery or serfdom (B.2).
Ethnic groups with distinctive cultural and territorial relationships, their economic systems representing primitive and nonurban economic arrangements (C.1).
Organized rule-governed physical competition, constituting recreation, sports, and games as a cultural element (D.6).

Communal events centered on religious, cultural, or civic identity, bridging religious belief and folklore (D.2) and recreation (D.6).
Legally enforceable rights over things, directly constituting property and property rights in economic systems (C.1.c).
Social-cultural systems of beliefs and practices relating to supernatural realities, constituting religious belief as a cultural element (D.2).
Structured system of grammar and vocabulary, constituting linguistic systems as a cultural component (D.5).
Social science studying production and distribution, providing the theoretical framework for analyzing types of economic systems (C).
Groups bound by shared language, culture, and ancestry, relevant to the varieties of groups and associations within societies (B.1).
Class struggle between bourgeoisie and proletariat directly addresses organization by status and class systems (B.2), and critique of capitalism frames types of economic systems (C).
Political and economic philosophy of social ownership, representing an alternative type of economic system (C).
Human between birth and puberty with progressive development, relevant to education and socialization as formal and informal enculturation (D.1).
Ethical and social tradition prioritizing hierarchical relationships and ritual practice, constituting religious belief and cultural values (D.2).
Labor theory of property rights (C.1.c) and social contract theory shaping legal and political institutions (D.3).
